perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that it will take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information, which measures shall include the highest degree of care that Recipient utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature. Recipient agrees to notify the Discloser in writing of any misuse or misappropriation of such Confidential Information which may come to its attention.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that it will take commercially reasonable measures, but no more than ordinary care, to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information. Recipient is not obligated to notify the Discloser of any misuse or misappropriation of such Confidential Information which may come to its attention.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the notification requirement regarding misuse or misappropriation of confidential information contradicts legal expectations of data breach reporting, especially in California where the agreement is governed.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1798.29 mandates that businesses notify individuals whose unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person. By explicitly stating the Recipient is not obligated to report misuse, the modified text directly contradicts this California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.8. PERSONAL DATA [1798 - 1798.78] ( Title 1.8 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) CHAPTER 1. Information Practices Act of 1977 [1798 - 1798.78] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) ARTICLE 7. Accounting of Disclosures [1798.25 - 1798.29] ( Article 7 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) 1798.29. (a) Any agency that owns or licenses computerized data that includes personal information shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach in the security of the data to any resident of California (1) whose unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person, or, (2) whose encrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the encryption key or security credential was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the agency that owns or licenses the encrypted information has a reasonable belief that the encryption key or security credential could
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text directly contradicts California Civil Code § 1798.29. The original text mandates notification of misuse or misappropriation of confidential information. The changed text explicitly states that the recipient is not obligated to report such misuse. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically CIV 1798.29(a), states that any agency owning computerized data including personal information shall disclose any breach of the security of the system. By removing the notification obligation, the modified contract fails to comply with this California law, resulting in a clear legal contradiction. The change removes a core legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Any materials or documents of which have been furnished by the Discloser to the Recipient will be promptly returned, accompanied by copies of such documentation, after the evaluation set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement has been concluded.
perturbation.changed_text: Any materials or documents which have been furnished by the Discloser to the Recipient will be returned within 180 days of the evaluation set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement, without copies.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the return of confidential materials and removing the requirement to provide copies may conflict with trade secret laws which emphasize the immediate return of proprietary information upon request or termination of an agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426.2 details misappropriation of trade secrets, which includes continued possession or use after the right to do so terminates. While not explicitly requiring immediate return, delayed return increases risk of trade secret misappropriation and contradicts expected reasonable measures to protect the trade secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by delaying the return of confidential materials and removing the requirement to provide copies, which conflicts with trade secret laws. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (California Civil Code § 3426.2) doesn't explicitly require immediate return, it addresses misappropriation of trade secrets, which includes continued possession after the right terminates. The original text ensures prompt return, reducing the risk of misappropriation, while the changed text allows for a delay of up to 180 days, increasing this risk. Furthermore, the removal of the requirement to return copies exacerbates the potential for misuse or unauthorized disclosure of the information. This constitutes a legally significant inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: The foregoing commitments shall survive any termination of discussions between the parties, and shall continue for a period of three (3) years following the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The foregoing commitments shall survive any termination of discussions between the parties, and shall continue for a period of one (1) year following the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality term to one year may be deemed insufficient for protecting certain types of trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the reasonable duration requirement under trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426, defines trade secret misappropriation, requiring reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Limiting the confidentiality term to one year might be considered insufficient effort for information with a longer commercial lifespan, potentially violating the implicit requirement of reasonable protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality term from three years to one year, which may be considered insufficient to protect trade secrets, creating a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation argues that this could violate the implicit requirement of reasonable protection under California Civil Code § 3426 because it might not constitute reasonable effort for information with a longer commercial lifespan. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 only cites the title of the act, the Law Explanation uses this as a basis to say that decreasing the time of confidentiality is not reasonable protection, especially if a trade secret is expected to be confidential for a longer period of time.

perturbation.original_text: The federal and state courts within the State of California shall have excl usive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The courts of Delaware shall have exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement, regardless of conflicting forum selection clauses in any other agreements.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying Delaware courts while the agreement is governed by California law may conflict with California's interest in adjudicating disputes arising from contracts formed and performed within the state, especially when there are California residents involved.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 410.30
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 410.30 allows California courts to stay or dismiss actions when, in the interest of substantial justice, the action should be heard in a forum outside California. Forcing all disputes to be heard in Delaware, when the contract is based in California, could be argued as unjust and contrary to California's established legal procedures, particularly if it disadvantages a California resident or business.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.30. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.30. (a) When a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. (b) The provisions of Section 418.10 do not apply to a motion to stay or dismiss the action by a defendant who has made a general appearance. (Amended by Stats. 1972, Ch. 601. Note: This version was suspended from Sept. 22, 1986, until Jan. 1, 1992, during operation of the temporary amendment by Stats. 1986, Ch. 968.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by specifying Delaware courts for dispute resolution, potentially conflicting with California's interests, especially if the contract is based in California and involves California residents. This contradicts CCP 410.30(a), which allows California courts to stay or dismiss actions if a forum outside the state is more appropriate in the interest of substantial justice. By mandating Delaware, the changed text disregards the possibility that a California court might be a more just forum, particularly for California residents.
